stem: A 36-year-old client is 12 hours post-tibial plateau reconstruction and has an active epidural for pain relief. Based on the documentation, determine the most likely condition and select two actions the nurse should take and two parameters the nurse should monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation (14:15): I'm calling about the client 12 hours post-tibial plateau reconstruction. I suspect silent acute compartment syndrome in his right leg, as the epidural is masking pain.
Background: He has a continuous Bupivacaine/Fentanyl epidural. His recovery was uneventful until 14:00.
Assessment: The right lower leg is now extremely tense and swollen. Intracompartmental pressure is 36 mmHg. Despite 0/10 pain, he has new motor weakness of 1/5 in the foot.
Recommendation: I request an immediate surgical evaluation for a fasciotomy. I will maintain the limb at heart level and continue neurovascular checks.

tabs.title: Anesthesia & Muscle Vitality Log
tabs.content:
Assessment ItemCurrent FindingInterpretation
Pain Score0/10 (Comfortable)Masked by Epidural
Fascial TensionBrawny/Tense; No 'give' on palpationHigh Internal Pressure
Manometry (Needle)36 mmHgCritical (> 30)
Toe MovementWeak (1/5)Motor deficit

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
14:00:
BP: 122/78 mmHg
HR: 82/min
RR: 18/min
Temp: 37°C (98.6°F)
SpO2: 98% on room air
Pedal Pulses: Bounding bilaterally

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Bupivacaine/Fentanyl epidural infusion at 8 mL/hr continuous.

hoverRationales.c1: This is incorrect. While post-operative edema is expected, an intracompartmental pressure of 36 mmHg combined with motor weakness indicates a pathological process, not just swelling with good pain control.
hoverRationales.c2: This is correct. Acute compartment syndrome occurs when swelling within a fascial compartment increases pressure, compressing nerves and blood vessels. The high pressure (36 mmHg) and motor weakness, despite masked pain from the epidural, confirm this diagnosis.
hoverRationales.c3: This is incorrect. Epidural anesthesia does not cause localized, tense, high-pressure swelling in a single limb compartment. The findings are specific to a post-traumatic vascular and cellular injury within the fascia.
hoverRationales.a1: This is a correct action. An intracompartmental pressure above 30 mmHg indicates severe tissue ischemia that requires urgent surgical decompression (fasciotomy). Notifying the surgeon immediately is critical to prevent permanent muscle and nerve damage.
hoverRationales.a2: This is a correct action. Positioning the limb at heart level (neutral position) optimizes arterial inflow and venous outflow. Elevating the limb would decrease arterial perfusion, while dependency would increase edema, both worsening the ischemia.
hoverRationales.a3: This is an incorrect action. Increasing the epidural rate would further mask any potential breakthrough pain, which is a critical sign. The client's problem is tissue ischemia, not inadequate analgesia.
hoverRationales.a4: This is an incorrect action. Massaging a limb with compartment syndrome can increase intracompartmental pressure and worsen tissue ischemia. The area should not be manipulated.
hoverRationales.p1: This is a correct parameter. Serial intracompartmental pressure readings track the progression of the syndrome. Delta pressure (diastolic BP - intracompartmental pressure) is a key indicator of limb perfusion; a value <30 mmHg suggests ischemia.
hoverRationales.p2: This is a correct parameter. Worsening motor function and diminished or absent distal pulses are key signs of progressive neurovascular compromise from increasing compartment pressure. These are critical indicators of worsening ischemia.
hoverRationales.p3: This is an incorrect parameter. Serum glucose is not directly related to the pathophysiology of acute compartment syndrome. Monitoring it is not a priority in this emergent situation.
hoverRationales.p4: This is an incorrect parameter. The Babinski sign assesses for upper motor neuron lesions in the central nervous system. It is irrelevant for diagnosing a peripheral neurovascular issue like compartment syndrome.
